data.title: The model bought from Huading may be fake: Unveiling the gray market chain of AI transfer stations
data.description:
You think you're coding with Claude Opus 4.6, but in the background it might be a low-end, 9-parameter domestic model. You think you're saving money, but every prompt you make is being archived and used to train competing models. You think you've found a replacement, but the money on your bill is flowing into a gray market industry that starts with credit card fraud. This isn't a conspiracy theory. An arXiv paper uses data to prove that 45.83% of the "top-tier models" you spent real money tuning fail authentication. Even more frightening is that this is hardly a secret within the industry. Bonus at the end: a community-verified 30-second quick detection method. First, let's clarify: What exactly is an AI transit station? On July 9, 2024, OpenAI officially cut off API services to mainland China and Hong Kong. In September 2025, Anthropic followed suit, completely banning Chinese-controlled companies from using the Claude API. Google's Gemini also imposes strict restrictions on Chinese IP addresses. For Chinese developers, the doors to directly using the world's top AI models have closed one after another. Thus, the "transfer station" came into being. In short, a transit station is essentially an intermediary agent—it claims to help you bypass regional restrictions and payment barriers, allowing you to call the APIs of models like Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini at a lower price. You only need to replace a base_url and API key; without changing a single line of code, you can "seamlessly access" the world's most powerful AI models. This sounds wonderful. But behind this "wonderful" facade lies a pitfall you can't imagine. What does a legitimate transit station look like? Let's take a look at OpenRouter. Before discussing the dark side, it's necessary to examine how a "legitimate transit station" does business, so you can see the vast difference. OpenRouter is currently the world's largest AI model aggregation platform, connecting over 300 models and more than 60 providers. Its business model is extremely transparent: it charges approximately 5% service fee on top of the official inference fee (customized solutions are available for large clients). Every penny you pay has a clear destination—the model call fee goes to the upstream vendor, and the difference goes to OpenRouter. In 2025, the company secured $40 million in Series A funding led by a16z and Menlo Ventures, valuing the company at $500 million, with an ARR of $5 million, representing a 400% year-over-year growth. Its core selling point is "routing"—one API key connects to all models, enabling intelligent failover, and with transparent pricing. If you call Opus 4.6, you get Opus 4.6. Similar legitimate channels include EdenAI and Azure OpenAI Service, which have formal commercial partnerships with model vendors and are subject to compliance regulations. However, a problem arises—OpenRouter began imposing "account-level" bans on Chinese users at the end of 2025, restricting the use of models from the OpenAI, Claude, and Google platforms. Legitimate channels are becoming increasingly limited for Chinese users. This has precisely fueled the rampant growth of "underground relay stations." Dissecting the Four-Layer Gray Industry Chain of Relay Stations Domestic AI relay stations are far more complex than simply "proxy forwarding." This has formed an extremely sophisticated gray industry chain. The low prices you see are just the tip of the iceberg—what lies beneath the surface is far dirtier than you imagine. At the very bottom: Credit card fraud. At the darkest level of this industry chain lies credit card fraud. Some people possess large numbers of overseas black market credit card numbers, using platforms like OpenAI and Anthropic to create accounts in bulk and obtain API credit limits without requiring real-name registration overseas. The actual cost of these accounts is close to zero—because the money is deducted from the stolen credit cards. When you cheer for prices "as low as 30% of the official price," have you ever wondered—how can this price be achieved? This isn't efficiency optimization, nor is it economies of scale; it's someone else "paying the bill" for you—that "someone" might be a victim of credit card fraud. The second layer: Web-based reverse engineering—the business of converting subscriptions to APIs. Slightly more "respectable" than fraud is Web2API reverse engineering—cracking web-based subscription services into API interfaces for sale. These intermediary sites don't use official APIs; instead, they reverse-engineer the web-based interaction protocols of products like Claude and ChatGPT, capture and parse the session authentication process, and encapsulate the web-based calls into pseudo-APIs compatible with OpenAI format. The specific operation is as follows: Register Plus/Pro member accounts in batches to build an "account pool," and then use a proxy server for load balancing to distribute user requests across different accounts. A ChatGPT Plus account, costing $20 per month, can be shared by 5 to 20 people, each paying only a few dollars. Moreover, all of this is supported by a mature open-source toolchain. One API (GitHub 31.2k stars) is currently the most mainstream API aggregation and management tool, supporting unified access to over 30 large models, providing a full suite of functions including load balancing, token management, and channel management, one-click Docker deployment, and the MIT open-source license. The New API (GitHub 24k stars) is a secondary development based on the One API, adding commercial features such as online payment, intelligent channel routing, and cache billing, and adopts the AGPL-3.0 license. More recently, Sub2API (GitHub 9.5k stars) has become even more popular. The project's name translates directly to "Subscription to API"—it specifically converts subscription accounts for products like Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini into API interfaces. The project supports multi-account management, intelligent scheduling, session persistence, concurrency control, and even a complete management dashboard. The project's README contains a small, honest statement: "Using this project may violate Anthropic's Terms of Service. All risks are borne by the user." These three projects combined have over 64,000 stars, forming a complete "transfer station infrastructure." Anyone can set up a fully functional API transfer service in hours—deployment tutorials are everywhere, and ads for "zero-threshold, monthly income exceeding 10,000 yuan" side hustles are ubiquitous in developer communities. The third layer: Industrialized harvesting of free quotas. The free trial quotas offered by AI vendors to new users have also been targeted by black market actors. Taking Cursor as an example, multiple open-source projects on GitHub achieve unlimited free trial quotas by resetting device fingerprints. These projects have received thousands of stars, forming a complete closed loop of "open-source tools attracting users, paid accounts generating revenue." Manus AI's invitation points system was also compromised—automatic registration scripts developed by black market operators are priced between 1580 and 3200 yuan, reducing the cost of acquiring points to "3300 points for only 0.5 yuan." More than 125 related fraudulent products appeared on e-commerce platforms at one point. The Fourth Layer: The "Legitimate" Resellers in Suits There's another type of reseller that takes a seemingly "compliant" route—claiming to reduce costs through large-scale procurement and resell API quotas at a discount lower than the official price. Some even claim "1 yuan = 1 dollar"—for an official API quota of $1, the reseller only charges 1 yuan, equivalent to one-seventh of the official price. But where does the discount come from? There are only a few possibilities: either the model has been compromised, or they're using the "cheap supply" from the previous three layers, or they're initially using low prices to attract users and burn money, then figuring out how to monetize once they've built a large user base—or they might simply disappear. When you see a product priced far below cost, remember this: if you can't find anyone else paying for it, you are. If the above is just "industry rumors," then the following section contains irrefutable academic evidence. In March 2026, a paper titled "Real Money, Fake Models: Deceptive Model Claims in Shadow APIs" was published on arXiv (paper number 2603.01919), providing the first systematic academic audit of AI intermediaries. The research team identified 17 Shadow API services and found that 187 academic papers used these intermediaries. They then conducted in-depth analysis of three representative services. The findings are alarming: 45.83% of the model endpoints failed identity fingerprint verification. Nearly half. The model you're calling and the one you think you're using are very likely not the same thing. The paper categorizes fraudulent methods into three types: "Substitution Type"—This type claims to provide a certain version of the Gemini model, but actually replaces it with another version. The fingerprint verification result is completely inconsistent with the claimed model identity, yet the seller charges a premium of up to 7 times the original price. "Hypocrisy Type"—This is the most outrageous. A user calls Claude Opus 4.6 (GPT-5 in the paper's example), and the price appears to be the same as the official price, but the actual returned model is GLM-4-9B—a small, open-source model with a completely different number of parameters and capabilities. You spend tens of dollars per million tokens, only to receive the output of a model that runs almost for free. "Resale for Profit" – This involves purchasing weak model calls cheaply from upstream suppliers, repackaging them as top-tier models, and selling them to profit from the price difference. The paper presents stark data: Users pay 100% of the official price, but actually receive only 38% to 52% of the model's value. In monetary terms: for every $14.84 spent, the actual service you receive is only worth $5.70 to $7.77; the rest goes into the pockets of the middleman. Even more dangerous is performance collapse. In MedQA (Medical Q&A) benchmarks, the performance of the Gemini-2.5-flash provided by the intermediary platform plummeted from the official 83.82% to 37.00%—a drop of 46 percentage points. The gap in LegalBench was as high as 40 to 43 percentage points. In Mathematical Reasoning (AIME 2025), the deviation reached 40 percentage points. Imagine: medical consultation code written using this "intermediary Opus," legal analysis run using this "intermediary GPT-5," and academic papers submitted using this "intermediary Claude"—their reliability might be less than that of a free small model. It is estimated that approximately 56 academic studies required redoing due to citations of the Shadow API, involving costs ranging from $115,000 to $140,000. The conclusion is straightforward: the Shadow API should not be used in any scenario requiring reliability. The paper reveals the severity of the problem. But for ordinary developers, a more pressing question is—is the relay station I'm using actually legitimate? Is your model genuine? Community Practical Detection Manual Since spoofing is so prevalent, is there a way for ordinary users to verify it themselves? The paper and the technical community provide a complete set of methods, from "instant testing" to "professional auditing." The following detection methods come from highly-rated practice posts and open-source tools in the X (Twitter) developer community and have been verified by a large number of users. Method Zero: 30-Second Quick Screening (Temperature Set to 0.01) This is the most widely circulated "demon-revealing" test in the community, a highly praised post from @billtheinvestor: Input this string of numbers: "5, 15, 77, 19, 53, 54", and let the model sort or select the maximum value. True Claude: Almost always gets 77. True GPT-5.4: Often gets 162 (add the numbers). If the results fluctuate wildly after 10 consecutive tests, the probability of it being fake is extremely high. The principle is simple: different models have different training data and fine-tuning styles for instructions, and will have fixed "behavioral fingerprints" when faced with such fuzzy instructions. Fake models will either answer incorrectly or give different answers every time. Auxiliary Check 1: Abnormal Token Consumption

Send a simple "ping" (e.g., just type "hi") and check the returned input_tokens. If it shows more than 200 tokens—90% likely fake. This means the intermediate layer is stuffing you with a huge number of hidden system prompts to override your commands.

Auxiliary Check 1: Abnormal Token Consumption

Send a simple "ping" (e.g., just type "hi") and check the returned input_tokens. If it shows more than 200 tokens—90% likely fake.

. This means the intermediate layer is stuffing you with a huge number of hidden system prompts to override your commands. Auxiliary Check 2: Rejection Style Judgment Ask a question that violates the rules (e.g., "How to make a bomb") and observe the rejection language: Real Claude: Polite but firm, "Sorry but I can't assist with that." Fake Model/Local Mini-Model: Frequently uses emojis, verbose tone, even saying "Sorry, Master~" Auxiliary Check 3: Functional Missing Point Check If the transfer station claims to be Opus 4.6/GPT-5.4, but: Function calling is not supported; image recognition is not possible; long contexts (e.g., 32k) are unstable. → This is most likely a weak model impersonating another. Method 1: Directly "interrogate" the model's identity. Although system prompts can forge identity claims, many low-quality intermediaries won't go this far. Directly ask "What model are you?" or "Please describe your training data deadline." If a model claiming to be Opus 4.6 gets its own basic information wrong, there's a high chance something's wrong. Method Two: Latency and Token Fluctuation Analysis The official API's inference latency and token count are relatively stable. However, if you find that the response time for the same question fluctuates wildly, or the output length fluctuates abnormally, it may mean that the backend model is being frequently switched—sometimes given a genuine model, sometimes a cheaper one. Send the same prompt repeatedly more than 10 times and observe the consistency of the response time and output content. Method Three: Capability Boundary Testing The difference between top-level models and smaller models is most obvious in complex inference tasks. Prepare several challenging math problems, logic puzzles, or professional domain problems with clear answers (such as AIME competition problems), and send the same request to both official channels and intermediary platforms to compare the quality of the answers. If a model claiming to be Opus 4.6 frequently fails on basic reasoning problems, it's likely not genuine. Method Four: LLMmap Fingerprint Recognition (Professional Level) This is the core method used in the paper—LLMmap is an active fingerprint recognition framework that sends 3 to 8 carefully designed queries to the model, analyzes the statistical characteristics of the responses (word frequency, sentence structure, specific expression habits), and calculates the cosine distance with a known model fingerprint database. Even if the model is disguised, this method can penetrate the disguise. In short: If a relay station is hesitant to let you run any of the above tests, or if the test results don't match the official ones—run them, and don't look back. Small-scale testing and recharging only after use is the most pragmatic self-preservation strategy at this stage. Every prompt you send is being sold for a price. If model manipulation is "giving you less," then data selling is "taking more from you." The technical essence of a relay station is a proxy layer—every prompt and every response you send passes completely through its server. The relay station operator can effortlessly obtain all the code, business plans, customer data, and private conversations you send. This is not a theoretical deduction. Extensive discussions within the developer community have long pointed out that it's an open secret that relay stations use user request data for model distillation. Model distillation, simply put, is using the output of a large model to train a smaller model—a technique of "stealing" skills. All requests that pass through the relay—the complete prompt and response—constitute a ready-made, high-quality training dataset. Especially the outputs of top-tier models like Opus 4.6 and GPT-5, which are themselves extremely valuable distillation corpora. In early 2026, Anthropic released a report directly accusing three Chinese AI labs—DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax—of using a network of fake accounts to massively access the Claude API for model distillation. MiniMax had over 13 million interactions, and Moonshot over 3.4 million. Their "Hydra cluster" architecture—a network of numerous fake accounts—is identical to the "account pool" model of relay stations. From a technical architecture perspective, relay stations are divided into "pure pass-through" (requests are forwarded in real-time, without disk write-to-disk) and "store-and-forward" (data is stored first, then forwarded). However, even for services claiming to be "pure pass-through," no one can audit whether data is actually stored in the backend. Your trust is entirely based on the verbal promises of an anonymous operator. Security experts recommend evaluating transit stations from five dimensions: whether the technical architecture is transparent, whether the logging policy only records billing metadata, whether transmission uses TLS 1.2+, whether the API key is completely isolated, and whether there is a leak emergency mechanism. However, in reality, the vast majority of domestic transit stations are not even transparent about their main information, let alone accept independent security audits. Relay stations also face a fatal systemic risk—running away. Most transit stations use a pre-paid model, where you pay first and then are charged based on usage. Once the operator disappears, your balance vanishes completely, and you have no way to hold them accountable. HodlAI is a textbook example: Initially, the project team generously offered low-priced APIs to attract users to deposit funds. However, when the national treasury funds dwindled to approximately $60,000 and daily token consumption reached $10,000, they began drastically tightening restrictions—capping single requests at 50,000 tokens and escalating frequency limits. When users questioned this in a Telegram group, they were immediately kicked out of the group and their accounts banned. Community comments were succinct: "Just like a pyramid scheme," "Keeping quiet is far easier than solving problems," and "The same old formula, the same old taste." Insiders summarize this model in one sentence: "First, attract users with low prices; once the user base is large, the upstream accounts are blocked, and they simply disappear. The only ones who suffer are the users." Similar posts seeking redress are commonplace in developer communities like Linux.do and V2EX. Some of these intermediary sites have extremely unfair contract terms, and some don't even have any business registration information. You don't even know who to sue. A complete industry chain: From black market SIM cards to your IDE. Putting all the above information together, you'll see a clear chain: Upstream ammunition—SMS verification platforms provide phone numbers, black market SIM card providers provide payment methods, and SIM card pools provide device resources. Midstream weapons—Reverse engineers crack protocols, open-source projects like One API/New API/Sub2API provide ready-made infrastructure, and device farms mass-produce accounts. Downstream distribution—Relay station operators package it as "API services" for sale, Telegram groups and e-commerce platforms become sales channels, and some even package "building a relay station" as a side hustle training course. And you—through IDE tools like Cursor and Claude Code, or through your own code—are the end consumer in this chain. Data from the security company Threat Hunter shows that of the 50 AI Agent products they sampled, every single one has black market derivative services. This industry chain has evolved from account trading in 2022, to API resale in 2023, to free quota arbitrage in 2024, to Agent computing power abuse in 2025, and by 2026—it has completed a full evolution from small-scale workshops to industrialized production. In conclusion, the story of the AI ​​transfer station is essentially an AI-era version of an old business logic—when you don't know what the product is, you are the product. Your money bought a fake model, your data was fed to someone else's training set, and your prepaid balance could go to zero at any time. These three things are not "possible," but "actually happening." A few practical suggestions—use official APIs whenever possible. Official APIs are expensive, but the price is justified. If your business has any requirements for data security and model reliability, a relay station should not be in your technology stack. At least learn self-testing. If you are using a relay station, run a test using the method described above. Compare the relay station's output with the official output using the same AIME math problem and the same complex code. If the difference is significant—you know what to do. Sensitive data should never pass through a relay station. If you must use it, at least do the following: de-identify sensitive information, rotate API keys regularly, and do not store any core data in the relay station account. Carefully examine domestically produced models. DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, and other domestic modelers are rapidly catching up in terms of capabilities, with transparent pricing and prices far lower than overseas models. Their official APIs can be used legally and directly in China. Instead of risking using adulterated overseas models in a gray area, it's better to use these legitimate domestic alternatives—at least you know what you're adjusting. This industry is changing every day. But one ironclad rule remains unchanged: Choosing the cheapest option without understanding the costs is often the most expensive decision.

data.title: From Guessing Games to Temporary Rules: A Decade of Absurdity in Crypto Regulation
data.description: On March 17th, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) released the long-awaited rulebook for the cryptocurrency industry since 2013. I am pleased with this and am working towards it. Bitcoin has fallen 44% from its October high. Ethereum is priced around $2,000, less than half its price seven months ago. The total market capitalization of altcoins has evaporated $470 billion since its peak. The Fear & Greed Index has reached 11. This isn't just a bad week; it's 11 out of 100. This means people have stopped arguing about where the bottom is and have started selling off their remaining cryptocurrencies. And just then, on March 17th, the SEC and CFTC released a document that finally reveals exactly what the tokens you hold are. Previously, the two sides had endured a decade-long legal battle, hundreds of enforcement actions, and billions of dollars in legal fees. Some companies even chose to relocate to Singapore rather than continue playing a guessing game with Gary Gensler. The answer was finally revealed the week Ethereum's price fell below $1900. But the key point is that while the token economy itself suffered a major blow, everything beneath it is thriving. The circulating supply of stablecoins has surpassed $316 billion, and the size of on-chain real-world assets (RWA) has reached $26.5 billion and continues to grow. As a result, Morgan Stanley is building a crypto trust bank. Meta abandoned its Metaverse project but is bringing stablecoins to WhatsApp. Stripe is processing $400 billion worth of stablecoin transactions. Nasdaq is building a tokenized stock trading platform. Cryptocurrencies are becoming a pillar of global finance, and in most cases, it doesn't rely on tokens. Cryptocurrencies are no longer merely a speculative asset class. The regulations issued on March 17th were originally designed for first-generation cryptocurrencies, but they were only formally implemented after the advent of the second generation of cryptocurrencies. However, this doesn't mean they are meaningless. As SEC Chairman Paul Atkins once said: "We are no longer the 'Securities and Everything Commission'." "Is this a bit late?" For the first time, US regulators have provided a unified definition of cryptocurrencies. Five categories, with each token falling into one. I will present these definitions below; please read with the assumption that you've never heard of these concepts before. Digital commodities are the main focus. A digital commodity is a crypto asset whose value derives from the programmed operation of a fully functional crypto system and supply and demand dynamics. Its value does not depend on the management of a central issuing authority. If the network is truly decentralized and functioning properly, without any single company backing it, then the asset is a commodity. This is governed by the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), not the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Sixteen mainstream tokens, including Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, Cardano, Avalanche, Polkadot, Chainlink, Dogecoin, and Shiba Inu Coin, have been officially recognized as digital goods. Dogecoin and Shiba Inu Coin meet this definition because no initiator or institution is driving their value growth. They lack promises, roadmaps, and ongoing team work crucial to their token value. This is why they are considered goods rather than securities. The criterion is whether someone promises a return based on their work. Digital securities refer to tokenized stocks, bonds, and government bonds. Simply put, these assets are securities before being put on the blockchain and remain securities afterward. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates these assets. It's that simple. Digital collectibles are NFTs tied to specific items or experiences. Digital tools are assets used to access software or services without expecting a return on investment. Stablecoins have their own category under the GENIUS Act. Staking, mining, and airdrops have all been approved. The ruling explicitly states that receiving mining rewards, participating in on-chain staking, or receiving digital goods airdrops do not constitute securities trading. This eliminates one of the biggest legal risks faced by proof-of-stake networks since the Gensler era. Packaging non-security tokens has also been approved. These 16 named tokens are all underlying infrastructure, backed by years of decentralized development. DeFi protocol tokens—such as JUP, POL, METEOR, and most tokens launched in the last two years—were not named and clearly do not meet the criteria. A fully functional crypto system without centralized regulatory oversight is a high bar. Most actively developed protocols do not meet this standard. This gray area, which this interpretation should address, remains unclear for the tokens actually held by most people. Value must derive from the programmatic operation of a fully functional system, not from someone's promise. This testing standard transforms a decade-long ambiguity into something compliance officers can actually tackle. There's more to this than meets the eye. This announcement does not constitute a formal rulemaking process under the Administrative Procedure Act, nor is it legally binding as a law or officially promulgated regulation. You'd better read that sentence again. This 68-page document we've been waiting for is merely an interpretative announcement, not a law or regulation, but simply an institutional position statement issued by the current chairmen of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), which they can withdraw at any time. This interpretation is a formal institutional action by the SEC and CFTC and is legally binding. However, in the absence of relevant legislation, future administrations can amend it. The document itself reserves the right for each agency to refine or expand its views. A future SEC chair with a differing political stance can overturn this interpretation without congressional approval. The next administration doesn't even need new laws, just new leadership. Atkins was well aware of this. He expressed this view on the day it was released, calling on Congress to take action to provide more lasting clarity. He saw this interpretation as a transitional measure, awaiting Congress to take action on comprehensive market structure legislation. This legislation is the Market Structure Transparency Act (CLARITY Act). Currently, the CLARITY Act is being considered in the Senate. The House of Representatives passed the CLARITY Act in July 2025 with 294 votes. Such high support from both parties indicates a genuine consensus. Then it went to the Senate and stalled. The key obstacle to the bill's passage is the yield of stablecoins. Banks believe that allowing crypto platforms to pay interest on stablecoin balances will trigger deposit outflows. People will withdraw money from their savings accounts and deposit it into USDC. To obtain higher returns, banking lobbying groups immediately began lobbying. The Senate Banking Committee canceled its planned January 2026 deliberations. The bill made no progress for the next two months. On March 20, Senators Tom Tillis and Angela Alsobrooks confirmed an agreement in principle on stablecoin rewards, which was supported by the White House. The agreement prohibited passive yields on stablecoins; rewards for activities linked to payments and platform usage were still allowed. Neither side was satisfied, and compromises are often made this way. But the yield agreement was only one of five things that needed to be completed before the CLARITY Act could take effect. The completion of the other four legislative steps came at a time of great tension this year. The Senate Banking Committee deliberations; and a full Senate vote (requiring 60 votes) Coordination with the Agriculture Committee and with the House version. Presidential signature. The Banking Committee's deliberations are scheduled for the second half of April, after the Easter recess. Senator Bernie Moreno warned that if the bill fails to reach the full Senate before May, digital asset legislation may not make progress for years. In addition, the Iran war also occupied a significant amount of Senate discussion time. There was also the voter identification bill that Trump indicated he wanted to pass first. Provisions regarding decentralized finance (DeFi) remain unresolved, with Senate Democrats expressing concern about the potential for illicit financial activities. Ethics provisions are also yet to be finalized, particularly whether senior government officials should be prohibited from profiting from crypto assets—a politically sensitive issue given the current administration's holdings of cryptocurrency. Senate Republicans are currently discussing attaching deregulation provisions for community banks as a political bargaining chip to the bill, which would trigger a new round of negotiations. The U.S. House Financial Services Committee recently held a hearing entitled "Tokenization and the Future of Securities: Modernizing Capital Markets." Witnesses included Kenneth Bentsen of the Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association (SIFMA), Summer Mersinger of the Blockchain Association, Christian Sabella of the DTCC, and John Zeka of Nasdaq. Zecca). Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange are both building tokenized stock trading platforms. DTCC handles the current settlement. If the DTCC endorses the efficiency of blockchain, then this debate is effectively over. Therefore, infrastructure construction is based on a rulebook that may not exist in two years. This is the dilemma the industry currently faces. Companies are making billions of dollars in decisions to build custody systems, tokenized platforms, and staking infrastructure, all based on a persuasive but not legally binding interpretative document. What is eternal, and what is not? For readers holding the aforementioned 16 tokens (such as ETH, SOL, XRP), these tokens are now officially recognized as digital goods under US law due to statements from two regulatory officials. This classification will remain in effect as long as these two officials or their successors maintain this designation. If CLARITY Once the bill is passed, it becomes law. No future president has the power to overturn it without congressional approval. The listed assets will be permanently defined, and the classification criteria will be binding. If it hasn't passed by May, the current classification system will depend on the opinion of a single government department. Currently, 16 named assets are temporarily safe, but not all assets are named. Most decentralized finance (DeFi), most new tokens, and any permissionless asset without a clearly defined issuer remain in a gray area, an issue not explicitly addressed in previous interpretations. The most anticipated sentence is like a pencil draft. Someone needs to pick up a pen and formally implement this. Everything depends on the Senate's actions over the next six weeks. Will these rules last long enough for all of this to make sense?

data.title: Stablecoin licenses are "late": Hong Kong's digital finance compliance is accelerating.
data.description:
March 2026 has passed, and the market's widespread expectation of the first batch of compliant stablecoin issuer licenses in Hong Kong has not materialized as scheduled. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) responded simply and firmly: it is making every effort to advance the licensing process and will announce it to the public in due course.

This change has led the market to re-examine the pace of Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation and has kept the outside world highly focused on who will ultimately receive the first batch of licenses.

On the surface, this is just a delay in time. However, if examined within the framework of virtual assets that Hong Kong is carefully constructing, the signals released by this delay are far more profound than the issuance time of a single license.

March Not Yet Here: Delay or "Deep Calibration"

Previously, market expectations and various "insider information" had placed banking giants such as HSBC and Standard Chartered in the spotlight for the first batch of licenses.

As note-issuing banks in Hong Kong, they possess inherent financial infrastructure attributes, a foundation of public trust, and a complex global risk control network. Standard Chartered has already appeared in the regulatory sandbox, and although HSBC has not publicly applied, it has always been considered the most significant potential candidate. However, with March ending and licenses still not issued, this delay usually points to several possibilities: The standards are not merely a "passing grade": Regulators are examining not only whether applicants meet the minimum requirements, but also the overall maturity and robustness of their issuance plans. Repeated refinement of details: This involves the composition and verification of reserve assets, the smoothness of the redemption mechanism, the security of the custody model, the transparency of information disclosure, and contingency plans for extreme scenarios. These details are the core of the stablecoin trust mechanism. "Examples" are more important than "speed": The first batch of licensed institutions will serve as models. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) is clearly more concerned with whether the system can withstand practical testing than with simply pursuing rapid implementation. It's worth noting that the HKMA communicated closely with potential applicants as early as the stablecoin sandbox phase. This reveals Hong Kong's regulatory philosophy: first, establish a closed loop on a small scale, then fully open it to the market. This seemingly conservative pace is actually to solidify the credibility of the entire system and avoid shaking the whole system due to problems with the first batch of institutions. Therefore, the subtle changes in the licensing timeline are no longer merely adjustments to administrative procedures, but are interpreted by the market as a barometer of regulatory standards, the rigor of approvals, and Hong Kong's ultimate attitude towards this field. Behind Hong Kong's caution: Stablecoins are by no means ordinary financial products. It is a crucial anchor connecting the on-chain crypto world with the off-chain real economy, spanning multiple dimensions such as payments, settlements, cross-border circulation, reserve asset management, anti-money laundering, and technological security. Its stability directly impacts the foundation of the digital financial ecosystem. Once stablecoins enter circulation, they will immediately face four real-world challenges: Are the reserves sufficient, verifiable, and quickly convertible? Are the redemption channels clear and efficient? What are the boundaries of responsibility in the event of systemic risk? How can cross-border flows seamlessly integrate with anti-money laundering measures and national regulations? These questions determine that stablecoins are a trust system requiring continuous maintenance. Hong Kong must ensure that this system gains international financial market recognition and can withstand the dual stress tests of the market and regulators. Therefore, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) prefers to refine the details of the system before issuing licenses rather than rushing its rollout. From this perspective, the "silence" in March may not be a bad thing; it's more like the regulators conducting a final stress test to ensure the carefully designed system can get off to a stable start. Furthermore, institutions such as Futu Securities and OSL Group are considered strong contenders for the second batch of licenses. This indicates that Hong Kong's goal is far more than simply introducing a few stablecoin projects; it aims to seize the right to define the rules for future digital financial infrastructure. The Regulatory Loop of CARF and CRS If stablecoin licenses are considered the "issuance end" of Hong Kong's virtual asset regulation, then the Crypto Asset Reporting Framework (CARF) and the revised Common Reporting Standard (CRS) constitute the "information end" and "tax end," respectively. The combination of these three elements is bringing digital assets into a traceable, reportable, and information-exchangeable global compliance network. According to Mr. Chan Ho-lim, Under Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury, Hong Kong plans to complete CARF legislation by 2026, implement a revised CRS by 2028, and conduct its first data exchange with partner jurisdictions by 2029. This timeline clearly declares that Hong Kong's regulation of virtual assets is no longer limited to the single dimension of exchange licensing, but is fully aligning with global tax transparency standards. For all market participants, such as platforms, custodians, asset management institutions, and even individual holders, future information disclosure obligations will be clearer, and compliance costs will increase accordingly. Hong Kong's virtual asset environment will therefore become more standardized, and the space for arbitrage using information asymmetry will be continuously compressed. This also illustrates that Hong Kong is forming a closed loop for virtual asset compliance: stablecoin licenses address issuance barriers, CARF and CRS address information transparency, and the combination of these three will make Hong Kong's digital finance ecosystem increasingly resemble the regulatory logic of traditional financial markets. Conclusion: Slow is Fast In the market's conventional understanding, delays in issuing licenses are often equated with "obstructed progress." However, in Hong Kong, an international financial center built on rules and credibility, "later" often does not mean slower; rather, it may indicate greater certainty and institutional resilience. The reason why the first batch of stablecoin licenses is worth waiting for is not because of their inherent mystery, but because they will likely establish the first gold standard for Hong Kong's stablecoin system. When CARF, CRS, stablecoin regulation, and virtual asset tax reporting are all in place and seamlessly integrated, Hong Kong's vision for the future of digital finance will truly reveal its solid outline.

data.title: AI and the Privacy Future of Encryption
data.description: In yesterday's article, I shared some of the risks that AI applications might pose to personal privacy that I encountered. The reason for this risk is the current operating mode of AI applications. When an end user calls upon AI, their data is directly uploaded to a large AI model located in the cloud. This large model then uses the data to perform inference and directly obtain the user's behavioral characteristics. The longer this data accumulates, the more comprehensively AI models can grasp users' behavioral habits through algorithms. This risk represents a privacy breach for individuals and a breach of trade secrets for companies. In an interview video last year about AI, Huang Renxun mentioned this risk and strictly required his company's employees to specify which data must be stored locally and which data could be uploaded to the cloud when using AI tools. However, at that time, I only heard about this risk as information; I only remembered his warning when I personally experienced it. This problem is only just emerging, but it will soon escalate. Therefore, I believe that the more widespread AI becomes in the future, the more we will need a terminal (whether it's a mobile phone, glasses, or some other form we cannot yet imagine) that runs a streamlined large model locally, processing most sensitive data and performing inferences for simple requirements locally, while only uploading heavy tasks and data, after "filtering," to the cloud for processing by complex large models. This avoids the large models in the cloud directly capturing users' individual behavioral characteristics. In the crypto ecosystem, privacy has long been a top priority. Earlier, Vitalik Buterin mentioned that the transparency of data and information on public blockchains like Ethereum hinders their widespread use in the commercial sector—because in commercial transactions, parties often prefer to protect their trade secrets and avoid disclosing transaction information. Recently, some business users have pointed out that they are quite cautious about the large-scale use of stablecoins. This is because stablecoin accounts on public blockchains are currently public, meaning anyone can see which account holds how much stablecoin. Once account identity information is leaked, the amount of funds (in stablecoins) held by which company becomes transparent. Therefore, whether in AI or the crypto ecosystem, privacy is a problem that must be addressed. However, if we carefully compare the applications of AI and the crypto ecosystem in terms of privacy, it seems that crypto applications are currently ahead—the crypto ecosystem has long had privacy coins (such as Monero, ZCASH, etc.) and coin mixers. But these privacy applications are actively avoiding regulation, so they are all somewhat negatively labeled. The most mature and feasible privacy applications that can truly gain public and regulatory acceptance are likely those based on zero-knowledge proofs. One method tested so far involves using a licensed regulatory body as an intermediary. Both parties conduct transactions through this intermediary, concealing their identities and transaction information, and only verifying the final zero-knowledge proof on a public blockchain. This ensures transaction privacy and avoids any suspicion of criminal activity for both parties. It is hoped that in terms of privacy protection and transaction compliance, exploration and application within the crypto ecosystem can leverage its own advantages to pave a new path for AI applications.

data.title: Is the Middle East war really coming to an end?
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>Everyone hopes the Middle East war will end as soon as possible. Trump's statement about withdrawing troops within three weeks, the confirmed date for his May visit to China, the passage of 10 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, the removal of the Iranian foreign minister and speaker from the strike list, and rumors of secret US-Iran contacts… all these signals point to a high probability of a short-term end to the Middle East war. The best time to end the war was yesterday, the second best time is now. For the Trump administration, there is no benefit in continuing the war. He is not faced with a "good or bad" choice, but rather "worse or worst." Only by resolving the conflict as quickly as possible can he prevent its spillover effects from impacting the midterm elections this November, and even further affecting the 2028 presidential election. The Strait of Hormuz and the Energy Game If the war truly comes to an end, what will become of the Strait of Hormuz? Will it be blocked indefinitely? Realistically, this possibility is not high. Even if the Iranian regime does not change, its overall strength will be significantly weakened after a round of military strikes, making it difficult to rely on a strait to confront the world in the long term. More importantly, this is not just a European problem. The one that will truly bear the brunt of the pressure first is likely to be one of Iran's most important buyers, China. Europe can still source energy from other regions, but China's dependence on the Strait of Hormuz is much greater. If the waterway is blocked for a long period, the pressure on China will be more direct. Therefore, a core variable in this matter is actually China's attitude, especially how China and the US communicate and coordinate, which could become a key factor influencing the subsequent situation. Meanwhile, the United States is significantly more resilient to pressure on this issue. In recent years, the localization of US energy production has increased dramatically, and it is no longer as heavily reliant on Middle Eastern crude oil as it once was. From a supply-side perspective, even if problems arise in the Strait of Hormuz, the direct impact on the US mainland would be relatively limited; the real impact would primarily be on European and Asian countries. Of course, there is also a more ambiguous, but equally realistic, scenario: Iran may not be able to completely blockade the strait, but might resort to "charging passage," effectively extorting passing oil tankers. This approach would also cause ongoing disruption. The United States has clearly stated that it should not accept such behavior, but "whether to accept" and "whether to prevent" are two different things. In this situation, responses from different countries are likely to diverge. For example, if Iran, in order to survive, might "let China off the hook" and allow it to sail, then trade routes and flows could be reshaped. Intermediate links such as transshipment, resale, and arbitrage could emerge, potentially leading to Chinese merchants smuggling cheaply purchased oil to Europe for huge profits, further complicating the issue. The Chaotic Iranian Regime The New York Times recently published a series of reports on Iran, including several journalists who have long studied authoritarian regimes. They made a key judgment: Iran is currently highly divided, with a blurred power structure, and to some extent, a situation where "no one truly makes the final decision." According to reports, during the large-scale protests in Iran in 2019, the Iranian regime was actually on the verge of collapse, its internal state extremely fragile, but this was unknown to the outside world. On the surface, Khamenei managed to "suppress" the situation through a series of measures, making the regime appear to have stabilized and successfully weathered the crisis. The problem lies in the fact that Khamenei was killed two months earlier in the US-Israeli joint strike, and whether his son, Mojtaba, can truly take over the mess amidst the gunfire and chaos is a question no one can answer definitively. Against this backdrop, Trump's strategy becomes clearer: he is not simply negotiating with a stable government, but rather attempting to identify, and even screen, the more "pro-American" or more cooperative faction within Iran. Once negotiations are reached, the US may use external forces to support this faction to come to power. Currently, the most prestigious "potential supporter" is Reza Pahlavi. Prince Pahlavi, in exile for forty years. In 1978, at the age of 17, Pahlavi went to the United States for pilot training. A year later, in 1979, the Islamic Revolution broke out, ending the "Pahlavi Dynasty" and the "Iranian Empire," and abolishing the monarchy. Following this, the regime changed, and the country became the "Islamic Republic of Iran." He never returned and settled in the United States. For the next forty years, as the exiled crown prince, he navigated the circles of Western think tanks and media, never leaving the Iranian political scene. A name without legitimacy leads to dissent; dissent leads to failure. When an old regime collapses and new powers rise, possessing the bloodline of the original dynasty is a significant political asset. Now, Pahlavi is experiencing the most representative "highlight" of his exile. Following the killing of Khamenei in a joint US-Israeli operation at the end of February, Pahlavi launched an intensive political mobilization in March. He has repeatedly stated that his goal is not necessarily to restore the monarchy, but to give the Iranian people the freedom to choose their political system. He says he will accept a republic if the people choose it. He frequently appears in Western media and think tank events, calling on Western countries to pressure the Iranian government and supporting human rights movements within Iran (such as the recent "Women's Lives, Liberty" protests). The most pivotal event was his speech at the CPAC (Conservative Political Action Conference) in Texas on March 28, 2026, and the support rally he held in Washington that same month. At CPAC, Pahlavi's speech was highly impactful, its core message being the deep integration of Iran's future with American values. He told the audience that a free Iran would no longer be a nuclear threat, no longer support terrorism, and no longer block the Strait of Hormuz. Furthermore, Iran would establish strategic partnerships with the United States and Israel, potentially bringing over $1 trillion in benefits to the US economy. At the end of his speech, he even mimicked Trump's slogan, uttering the line that ignited the crowd: "President Trump is making America great again, and I'm going to make Iran great again. MIGA." He also deliberately addressed the biggest concerns from the outside world. He said Iran is not Iraq, and he would not repeat the mistakes of the "de-Baathist" campaign of the past, nor would he allow the power vacuum to escalate into anarchy. He promised to retain the existing bureaucracy and some military facilities, only eliminating theocracy at the top. Western media's portrayal has also quietly shifted this month. Fox News and The Jerusalem Post no longer describe him as a "former crown prince," but rather as "the leader of the Iranian opposition." Some Iranian Americans rallied at Copley Square, calling for the collapse of the Islamic Republic of Iran. "Across cities, generations, and social classes, Pahlavi has become the only widely recognized and truly legitimate opposition figure, his name chanted throughout the country," an article in *The Jerusalem Post* noted. "For many Iranians, he is more than just one of many political options. He represents a clear break with the Islamic Republic and a link to the continuity of Iran's national identity." Pahlavi is not merely a symbol; he has done a great deal of substantive preparation over the past two years. In April 2025, he officially launched the "Iran Prosperity Project," a 170-page operational manual for regime transition, compiled over several years by more than 100 experts. Its core logic is to shift the focus from "how to overthrow" to "what to do from day 1 to day 180 after the overthrow," including lifting sanctions, recovering $120 billion to $150 billion in frozen assets overseas, rebuilding energy supplies, consolidating the military, and holding a national referendum. His aim is to prevent Iran from falling into anarchy like Iraq or Libya after the regime collapse. In October 2025, he released the accompanying digital mobilization platform, "We Take Back Iran." According to his team, by early 2026, tens of thousands of active-duty Iranian security forces, police, and government personnel had registered through the platform, indicating their willingness to defect in the event of regime change. The core political gamble in Pahlavi's plan to "take back Iran" was to call for the defection of the Iranian regular army (Artesh). This armed force, with approximately 350,000 personnel, exists in a parallel structure to the Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) but has long been marginalized. The long-standing internal conflicts within Iran's military are another key point of entry for regime change in Iran. Within Iran, a highly militarized theocratic state, the antagonism between the Armed Forces (Artesh) and the Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) is not a recent phenomenon, but rather a structural cancer sown since the establishment of the regime in 1979. These two armed forces are fundamentally different in their bloodline and spirit. The Armed Forces are Iran's long-established regular army; their professional traditions, military doctrines, and even the family memories of many senior generals can be traced back to the more secular and nationalist Pahlavi dynasty. For them, protecting the "land of Darius and Cyrus" is paramount. The Revolutionary Guard, on the other hand, is a "private army" established by Khamenei and his predecessors to consolidate their power. Therefore, the Revolutionary Guard not only controls Iran's most elite missile forces and its most lucrative secret overseas accounts, but also monopolizes the country's construction, telecommunications, and energy industries through its vast commercial empire. In Tehran, the capital of Iran, a mid-level officer in the Revolutionary Guard might own a luxury mansion in the northern part of the city, while a colonel in the regular Iranian Armed Forces might be struggling to afford basic medical insurance for his entire family. This tension has escalated to a critical point during the 2026 war. According to battlefield reports from mid-March 2026, the Iranian Armed Forces undertook a significant amount of frontline air defense and territorial defense duties in response to external air attacks, but supplies were extremely scarce. Reports indicate that the Revolutionary Guard, controlling logistical lifelines, refused to provide medical evacuations for wounded Iranian Armed Forces soldiers and even intercepted ammunition. This has provoked considerable anger within the Iranian Armed Forces. There are indications that the US military is maintaining informal communication with the Iranian Armed Forces leadership through Qatar. These analyses all ultimately point to the fact that in Iran, currently fragmented into various states, the US military is identifying, waiting for, and assisting the most suitable "local regime" to regain control. The real pressure of the US midterm elections means the echoes of war will eventually reach the most practical places: gas stations. As the midterm elections approach, the negative feedback effects of the Iran war on US domestic politics are becoming apparent. A crucial variable is that support for the Iran war within the US was already low. This is a point many analysts have consistently criticized Trump for; the public relations for this war were essentially ineffective, and it could be said that an effective narrative was never established from the beginning. For ordinary Americans, they may not care about the complex logic of geopolitics, but they are very concerned about their cost of living, such as gas prices. Therefore, information is layered. For some who follow the news or are already Trump supporters, the war might seem "important" on a macro level, related to global affairs, energy, and geopolitics. But for most ordinary Americans, the impact is very concrete: an extra $100 a week on gas is more immediate than any grand narrative. Gas prices in many places have already reached $3.80, and in some areas even exceed $4 a gallon. In this context, Trump's emphasis on "this is short-term pain" is logically sound, but psychologically difficult for voters to accept. For most people, short-term pain is precisely the clearest and hardest to ignore. Whether this will translate into votes is still too early to judge. But what is certain is that inflation is eroding trust in the government, and the "kitchen economy" is once again becoming a decisive factor. From a congressional perspective, the direct impact of the war itself is limited. Influenced by economic factors such as rising oil prices, the Republican Party might lose the House of Representatives if the election were held now. However, with seven months until the midterm elections and the war still ongoing, the situation remains uncertain. Furthermore, anti-war sentiment within the United States has not formed an overwhelming consensus; those opposed to the war have not mobilized strongly, and those who are not opposed are not particularly resolute. This "middle ground" is unlikely to translate into a dramatic swing in the vote. A truly meaningful analysis will likely have to wait until June or July, breaking down the approximately 20 to 25 key swing seats one by one, to arrive at a relatively reliable assessment. While the Republicans face the risk of losing the House, the Senate situation is much more stable. If the Democrats want to truly change the situation, they need to win at least four more seats on top of their existing ones to have a substantial advantage. Winning three seats is not very meaningful, because in a 50-50 tie, the vice president's vote can break the deadlock. Therefore, given the current state structure, it will be very difficult for the Democrats to win the Senate. In states like Texas and Alaska, the Democrats have virtually no realistic chance of winning. Relatively speaking, swing states like New Hampshire have a better chance, as there is some uncertainty. North Carolina may also become a key battleground for the Democrats. Overall, the Democrats' theoretical "ceiling" is four seats, but realistically, they are more likely to gain one or two seats, and we are far from entering the most intense stage. Many states are still holding their primaries; for example, in Texas, the Democratic candidates themselves lack sufficient testing, and their past statements are constantly being dug up, all of which weaken their competitiveness. In the latter half of the 2028 election, a "divided Congress" scenario is expected: Republicans will control the Senate to retain control over personnel appointments and foreign policy, while Democrats, even if they regain control of the House, will face a "policy vacuum" due to legislative stagnation. During this period, large-scale domestic stimulus packages will be stalled due to the difficulty in passing fiscal subsidies. While this political deadlock will reduce government efficiency, from a macroeconomic perspective, it may actually strengthen US policy through unilateral executive orders, maintaining extremely strong consistency in core areas such as energy extraction and border security. Financial Market Repricing Amid the current turmoil in Iran, global macroeconomic asset valuation models are undergoing profound restructuring. The core variable in this repricing lies in the fact that the US is using its energy advantage to conduct a targeted harvesting and redistribution of global wealth. The crude oil market exhibits an extreme asymmetry: in the short term, fears of supply disruptions have supported oil prices fluctuating at historical highs, but savvy investors have already begun pricing in a post-conflict "supply glut." With the US domestic production capacity reaching its limit and Venezuela's development rights being reactivated, a Western-led new energy supply order is taking shape, meaning that the market power of Middle Eastern crude oil is facing a permanent dilution. In the currency market, the dollar's hegemonic position has not only not been weakened by the turmoil, but has been reinforced. In contrast, the euro is trapped in a long-term depreciation cycle due to energy shortages and political divisions. France and Spain's shirking of responsibility in military action not only exposed the weakness of European defense but also severely damaged market confidence in the euro. Because Europe lacks a deep energy moat like the US, this lack of economic sovereignty is translating into a currency disaster. Under the potential influence of fiscal plans such as the "Save America Act," global capital may accelerate its return to the US, seeking a safe haven amidst the geopolitical storm. The rise in gold prices in this scenario is driven by three overlapping factors: First, geopolitical risk premium. Before the Pahlavi regime truly consolidates its power, there will inevitably be a vacuum period. No one knows what Iran will ultimately become; as long as the situation remains unresolved, the Revolutionary Guard is not completely dismantled, remnants remain, and regional proxies are active, gold will maintain its high level. This driving force will continue until the situation becomes truly clear. Second, structural pressure on the dollar's credibility. Even if the Pahlavi regime is ultimately established and the petrodollar system expands, before that, the US has already experienced a costly war, a rebound in inflation, and renewed questioning of the sustainability of US finances. In this process, gold plays the role of a "fiat currency credit hedge," not just a tool for hedging geopolitical risks. Third, the structural trend of global central banks purchasing gold. This trend has been established since 2022, and the Middle East wars will only accelerate it, not reverse it. The impact on Bitcoin needs to be considered from two dimensions. The first dimension is liquidity. Falling oil prices, declining inflation, and the Fed opening up room for interest rate cuts create a macroeconomic environment of renewed liquidity easing. Historically, Bitcoin has been one of the biggest beneficiaries of every Fed easing policy, as its sensitivity to liquidity far exceeds that of any traditional asset. In this respect, Bitcoin is a clear beneficiary. Bitcoin's correlation with the Nasdaq has been quite high over the past few years. Every time global risk premiums surge—whether it's the impact of the pandemic in March 2020, the interest rate hike cycle in 2022, or any major geopolitical event—Bitcoin has not exhibited its theoretically expected "safe-haven asset" attributes; instead, it has fallen along with risky assets, and often by a larger margin. The reason is straightforward: Bitcoin's marginal holders are currently still institutional and retail investors with high risk appetites. When liquidity tightens, they prioritize selling the most volatile assets to obtain cash. Bitcoin happens to be the most volatile asset in their holdings. Therefore, in the initial stage of a war, soaring oil prices, and a collapse in global risk sentiment, Bitcoin will likely fall along with the Nasdaq, and possibly even more sharply. This is not a logical contradiction, but rather determined by market structure. The key variable for Bitcoin is not the war itself, but the Federal Reserve's reaction path. If soaring oil prices force the Fed to tighten liquidity again, Bitcoin will fall in the short term along with risk assets, and the decline could be quite dramatic. However, if the Fed is forced to compromise between inflation and recession, choosing to maintain loose monetary policy or even restart QE, Bitcoin will be one of the most direct beneficiaries.</div></div>

data.title: OpenAI Raises $122 Billion in Record-Breaking Funding Round at $852 Billion Valuation With Amazon, Nvidia, and SoftBank Leading
data.description:
OpenAI Secures $122 Billion Funding at $852 Billion Valuation in Record-Setting Round

OpenAI has raised US$122 billion in its largest financing round to date, valuing the company at US$852 billion and setting a new benchmark in Silicon Valley fundraising.

The injection comes as the AI developer accelerates its expansion into chips, data centres, and top talent, while preparing for a potential public listing.

Today, we closed our latest funding round with $122 billion in committed capital at an $852B post-money valuation.

The fastest way to expand AI’s benefits is to put useful intelligence in people’s hands early and let access compound globally.

This funding gives us resources to…

— OpenAI (@OpenAI) March 31, 2026

Amazon, Nvidia, and SoftBank Lead Massive Investment

The bulk of the funding came from major tech players, with Amazon.com committing US$50 billion, Nvidia US$30 billion, and SoftBank US$30 billion.

A notable portion of Amazon’s contribution, US$35 billion, is contingent on OpenAI either going public or achieving artificial general intelligence milestones.

This OpenAI round is so unhinged

> $122B Raised (Largest private round ever)
> Amazon puts in $50B → OpenAI signed a $100B AWS deal.
> NVIDIA puts in $30B → OpenAI runs on NVIDIA GPUs.
> SoftBank put in $30B → They're co-building Stargate together.

$35B of Amazon's… https://t.co/l6eYlBeEkqpic.twitter.com/dpX9aw3JTS

— Josh Kale (@JoshKale) March 31, 2026

SoftBank co-led the round alongside Andreessen Horowitz, D.E. Shaw Ventures, MGX, TPG, and T. Rowe Price, while Microsoft also participated without disclosing its investment size.

Additional backing came from BlackRock, Blackstone, Sequoia Capital, Thrive Capital, and Temasek, reflecting broad institutional confidence in OpenAI’s growth trajectory.

How Individual Investors and ETFs Are Getting Involved

For the first time, OpenAI raised over US$3 billion from individual investors through bank channels.

The company will also be included in several exchange-traded funds managed by ARK Invest, allowing retail investors greater access to its private shares ahead of an anticipated IPO.

Revenue Momentum and Enterprise Growth

OpenAI reported generating US$2 billion in monthly revenue and US$13.1 billion in total revenue last year, with enterprise sales now making up 40 per cent of the total and projected to reach 50 per cent by year-end.

NEWS: OpenAI just announced that it has officially closed their latest funding round with $122 billion in committed capital at a post money valuation of $852 billion.

"We are now generating $2B in revenue per month. At this stage, we are growing revenue four times faster than… pic.twitter.com/J1N1zebz1D

— Sawyer Merritt (@SawyerMerritt) March 31, 2026

While unveiling its plans for a unified “SuperApp” combining ChatGPT, coding tools, and web browsing, the company noted,

“Users do not want disconnected tools. They want a single system that can understand intent, take action, and operate across applications, data, and workflows.”

The company’s advertising pilot in ChatGPT has already produced an annualised US$100 million in revenue after just six weeks, marking a new revenue stream beyond its traditional subscription model.

How OpenAI Plans to Spend Its Capital

OpenAI has committed to investing more than US$1.4 trillion in physical infrastructure to support AI software in the coming years.

The financing will provide flexibility to expand computing resources and pursue its AI roadmap amid broader market uncertainty, including geopolitical tensions in Iran.

The company also extended its revolving credit facility to approximately US$4.7 billion, although it remains undrawn, signalling preparation rather than immediate liquidity needs.

Competition and Product Streamlining

Facing rising competition from rivals such as Anthropic, OpenAI has narrowed its product focus, discontinuing the Sora video generator and integrating video capabilities into ChatGPT.

Its Codex coding agent now serves over 2 million weekly users, a fivefold increase over three months, and its latest model, GPT-5.4, handles more than 15 billion tokens per minute.

OpenAI's Codex is becoming increasingly popular:

3x user growth and 5x usage increase since the start of the year, and over 2 million weekly active users.

The battle between Claude and Codex is intensifying, because, as Dario already said: being the best AI company with the… https://t.co/kZ6EDRVD9Epic.twitter.com/GwsDbnazam

— Chubby♨️ (@kimmonismus) March 19, 2026

CEO Sam Altman faces pressure to justify the enormous valuation as OpenAI positions itself for a 2026 IPO, even as it retreats from some high-spending initiatives.

OpenAI’s chief financial officer, Sarah Friar, described being “public-company capable” as “good hygiene” and a potential “trust-building moment” for the business.

User Base and Market Influence

ChatGPT supports more than 900 million weekly active users, including over 50 million subscribers, with search usage nearly tripling over the past year.

OpenAI claims that its revenue is growing four times faster than legacy tech giants from the internet and mobile eras, including Alphabet and Meta.

The company’s strategic partnerships, particularly with Amazon for cloud hosting and distribution, also include revenue-sharing agreements to support enterprise customers.

The record-breaking round reflects OpenAI’s effort to solidify its public market narrative, build infrastructure for long-term AI growth, and broaden access to its private shares ahead of a potential IPO.

data.title: Seven co-founders discussed how Anthropic came about.
data.description:
Compiled & Translated by: Deep Tide TechFlow

Guests: Anthropic Co-founders Chris Olah, Jack Clark, Daniela Amodei, Sam McCandlish, Tom Brown, Dario Amodei, Jared Kaplan

Podcast Source: Anthropic

Original Title: Building Anthropic | A conversation with our co-founders

Broadcast Date: December 20, 2024

Over the past week, Anthropic has suffered two major incidents:

First, nearly 3,000 internal documents were publicly accessible due to a CMS configuration error. Then, Claude Code v2.1.88 was released on npm with a 59.8MB source map, leaving 510,000 lines of source code exposed.

It's incredibly ironic that a company with "security" in its DNA has suffered such a series of operational mishaps.

But before rushing to ridicule, let's revisit an internal conversation between Anthropic's seven co-founders over a year ago.

This podcast episode, recorded in December 2024, features seven people discussing how the company was founded, how the Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) was developed, why the word "safety" shouldn't be used casually, and CEO Dario's frequently quoted statement: "If a building's fire alarm goes off every week, it's actually a very unsafe building." Hearing this statement now, it certainly carries a different meaning. Seven co-founders, quick recognition: Dario Amodei | CEO, former VP of Research at OpenAI, with a background in neuroscience, the final decision-maker for Anthropic's strategy and safety roadmap. He spoke the most in this conversation. Daniela Amodei | President, Dario's sister. She previously worked at Stripe for five and a half years, leading the Trust and Security team, and earlier in the nonprofit and international development fields. She is primarily responsible for the organizational structure and external communication of Anthropic. Jared Kaplan | Physics professor turned AI researcher, one of the core authors of *Scaling Laws*. He often provides judgments from an outsider's perspective, claiming he initially pursued AI because he was "tired of doing physics." Chris Olah | A leading figure in interpretability research, he entered the Bay Area AI scene at 19, working at Google Brain and OpenAI. He is the most technologically idealistic person in Anthropic. Tom Brown | First author of the GPT-3 paper, now managing Anthropic's computing resources. His perspective leans towards engineering and infrastructure; in the podcast, he discussed his journey from "not really believing AI would develop so quickly" to changing his mind. Jack Clark, former Bloomberg technology reporter and head of policy and public affairs at Anthropic, moderated the discussion, handling transitions and follow-up questions. Sam McCandlish, co-founder of the research team, spoke the least but often hit the nail on the head, serving as a "finishing touch."

Key Insights Summary

Why Do AI: From Being Bored with Physics to "Believe It When You've Seen Enough"

Jared Kaplan: "I did physics for a long time, got a little bored, and wanted to work with more friends, so I did AI."

Dario Amodei: "I don't think I explicitly convinced you; I just kept showing you AI model results. At some point, I showed you enough, and you said, 'Hmm, this looks right.'"

Jared Kaplan: "Many AI researchers were psychologically hurt by the AI ​​winter, as if having ambition was not allowed."

Dario Amodei: "My deepest lesson from the past decade is that many 'everyone knows' consensuses are actually herd mentality disguised as maturity. You've seen consensus overturned overnight a few times, and then you'll say: No, we'll bet on this. Even if you're only 50% right, you'll contribute a lot that others haven't."

Security and Scalability are Intertwined

Dario Amodei: "One of our motivations for scaling the model was that the model had to be smart enough for RLHF to work. This is what we still believe now: security and scalability are intertwined."

RSP, Responsible Scaling Policy is Anthropic

Anthropic

Tom Brown: "The RSP is like our constitution to Anthropic. It's a core guiding document, so we're willing to invest a lot of time and effort in refining it." Dario Amodei: "The RSP will prevent projects that don't meet safety standards from proceeding. We're not just talking slogans; we're genuinely integrating safety into every aspect of our work." Daniela Amodei: "We can't just use the word 'safety' to dictate our work. Our real goal is to make sure everyone clearly understands what we mean by safety." Dario Amodei: "What truly undermines safety is often frequent 'safety drills.' If a building has fire alarms going off every week, it's actually a very unsafe building." "Noble Failure" is a Trap Chris Olah: "There's a saying that the most ethical behavior is to sacrifice other goals for safety, demonstrating one's purity of purpose. But this approach is actually self-defeating because it allows decision-making power to fall into the hands of those who don't prioritize safety." Co-founders Pledge to Donate 80% of Income Tom Brown: "We've collectively pledged to donate 80% of our income to causes that advance society, something we all wholeheartedly support." Nobody Wanted to Start a Business, But We Felt It Had to Sam McCandlish: "Actually, none of us initially intended to start a company. We just felt it was our responsibility because it was the only way to ensure that AI development was heading in the right direction." Daniela Amodei: "Our mission is both clear and pure, which is rare in the tech industry." Explainability: A Whole "Artificial Biology" Hidden in Neural Networks Chris Olah: "Neural networks are incredibly beautiful, with so much beauty we haven't yet seen. I sometimes imagine walking into a bookstore ten years from now and buying a textbook on neural network biology, filled with amazing content." AI for Enhancing Democracy, Not as a Tool of Authoritarianism Dario Amodei: "We worry that if AI is developed incorrectly, it could become a tool of authoritarianism. How can AI be a tool for promoting freedom and self-determination? This area is just as important as biology and explainability." From White House Meetings to the Nobel Prize: AI's Influence Has Long Exceeded the Tech Circle Jared Kaplan: "2018 "You wouldn't have imagined the president calling you to the White House to say they're focusing on language models." Dario Amodei: "We've already seen the Nobel Prize in Chemistry awarded to AlphaFold; we should strive to develop tools that can help us create hundreds of AlphaFolds." Why study AI? Jack Clark: Why did we start doing AI? Jared: Why did you do AI? Jared Kaplan: I'd been doing physics for a long time, got a bit bored, and wanted to work with more friends, so I did AI. Tom Brown: I thought Dario convinced you. Dario Amodei: I don't think I explicitly "convinced" you. I just kept showing you AI model results to show you that they're general and not just applicable to a single problem. At some point, I showed you enough, and you said, "Hmm, this looks right." Jack Clark: Chris, when you were doing interpretability research, did you meet everyone at Google? Chris Olah: No. Actually, I met many of you when I first came to the Bay Area when I was 19. I met Dario and Jared then; they were postdocs, and I thought they were really cool at the time. Later, at Google Brain, after Dario joined, we sat side-by-side for a while. I also worked with Tom, and then at OpenAI, I worked with all of you. Jack Clark: I remember meeting Dario at a conference in 2015 and wanting to interview you. The Google PR person even said they needed to read all your papers first. Dario Amodei: I was writing "Concrete Problems in AI Safety" at Google at the time. Sam McCandlish: Before I started working with you, you invited me to your office for a chat, which was like a comprehensive overview of AI. I remember after that conversation I thought, "This is much more serious than I realized." You talked about "large computational blobs," the number of parameters, and the scale of neurons in the human brain.

Breakthrough Expansion

Jack Clark: I remember when we were working on scaling laws at OpenAI, scaling the model really worked at first, and it worked consistently and strangely well on many projects, from GPT-2 to scaling laws to GPT-3, we just kept getting closer and closer like that.

Dario Amodei: We were the group of "getting things done."

Jared Kaplan: We were also very excited about security. At the time, we had this idea: AI would be very powerful, but it might not understand human values, or even be able to communicate with us. Language models, to some extent, ensure that it understands a lot of implicit knowledge.
