Dario Amodei: There's also RLHF on top of language models. One of our motivations for scaling up the model was that the model had to be smart enough for RLHF to work. That's what we still believe: security and scaling are intertwined. Chris Olah: Yes, scaling was actually part of the security team at the time. Because we felt that to get people to take security seriously, you first need to be able to predict AI trends. Jack Clark: I remember at a UK airport, I sampled GPT-2 data to write fake news, and then sent it to Dario on Slack saying, "This could really work, it could have huge policy implications," and I remember Dario replying, "Yes." We did a lot of release-related work after that; it was crazy. Daniela Amodei: I remember that release; that was the first time we really started collaborating, with the release of GPT-2. Jack Clark: I think that was very helpful for us. We first did something "a little weird but safety-oriented" together, and then we did Anthropic, a larger-scale, equally weird but safety-oriented project. The Early Stages of AI Tom Brown: Back to the "Concrete Problems" paper. I joined OpenAI in 2016, and we were among the earliest members. I felt that paper was like the first mainstream AI safety paper. How did it come about? Dario Amodei: Chris knows, he was involved. We were at Google then, and I've forgotten what my main project was at the time; this feels like something I procrastinated on. We wanted to write down what open questions existed in AI security. Back then, AI security was often discussed in a very abstract way, and we wanted to ground it in real-world ML. This line of work has been going on for six or seven years now, but back then it was just a strange idea. Chris Olah: I think it was almost a political project in a sense. Many people didn't take security seriously back then. We wanted to compile a list of reasonable questions that everyone agreed on, many of which already existed in the literature, and then find credible people from across institutions to co-author the papers. I remember spending a long time communicating with more than twenty researchers at Brain to garner support for publication. If we only look at the problem itself, it might not all hold true in retrospect, and it might not be the most accurate question. But if we see it as consensus building: proving that "there are real problems here that deserve serious attention," then it's a crucial moment. Jack Clark: Ultimately, you'll enter a very peculiar science fiction world. I remember Anthropic talking about Constitutional AI early on, and Jared said, "We write a constitution for a language model, and its behavior changes." It sounded crazy at the time. Why do you think it's feasible? Jared Kaplan: I discussed this with Dario for a long time, and I think simple methods in AI often work extremely well. The earliest version was quite complex, but it was continuously simplified until it became: leveraging the model's strength in making choices, giving it explicit hints about what to look for is enough, and then we can write down the principles directly. Dario Amodei: This brings us back to "The Big Blob of Compute," "The Bitter Lesson," and "The Scaling Hypothesis": If you can give AI a clear goal and data, it can learn. A set of instructions, a set of principles—the language model can read them and compare them to its own behavior; the training objective is there. So Jared and I thought: it's possible, as long as the details are fine-tuned repeatedly. Jared Kaplan: It was strange for me in the early days. I came from physics, and now everyone is excited about AI, making it easy to forget the atmosphere back then. When I talked to Dario about these things, I felt that many AI researchers were psychologically hurt by the AI ​​winter, as if "ambition" was not allowed. Discussing security required first believing that AI could be very powerful and very useful, but at the time there was a kind of anti-ambition ban. One advantage physicists have is "arrogance"—they often do very ambitious things and are used to talking about grand visions. Dario Amodei: I think that's true; there were many things that couldn't be said in 2014. This is also a common problem in academia; except for certain fields, institutions are increasingly risk-averse, and industrial AI has inherited this mentality. I think it didn't emerge until around 2022. Chris Olah: There are also two forms of "conservatism": one is taking risks seriously, and the other is viewing taking things seriously and believing that an idea can succeed as arrogance. We were dominated by the latter at the time. A similar situation occurred in the 1939 nuclear physics discussions: Fermi was resistant, while Szilard or Teller took the risks more seriously. Dario Amodei: My deepest lesson from the past decade is that many "everyone knows" consensuses are actually herd mentality disguised as maturity. You've seen consensus reverse overnight several times, and then you'll say: No, we'll bet on this. It might not be right, but ignore the noise and bet. Even if you're only 50% right, you'll contribute a lot that others haven't. Jared Kaplan: This is also true today on some security issues: the outside consensus is that many security problems don't naturally emerge from technology, but our research at Anthropic shows that they do indeed emerge naturally. Daniela Amodei: But this has changed over the past 18 months, and the world's sentiment towards AI has also changed significantly. When we do user research, we hear more from ordinary users about their concerns about the overall impact of AI on the world. Sometimes it's about jobs, biases, toxicity; sometimes it's about "whether it will disrupt the world and change the way humans collaborate," which I didn't entirely anticipate. Am McCandlish: For some reason, the ML research community is often more pessimistic than the general public about "AI becoming very powerful." Jared Kaplan: In 2023, Dario and I went to the White House. During the meeting, Harris and Raimondo basically said: "We're watching you. AI is a big deal, we're paying close attention." But in 2018, you wouldn't have imagined "the president calling you to the White House to say they're looking at language models." Tom Brown: Interestingly, many of us entered the field when it seemed uncertain, like Fermi's skepticism about the atomic bomb. There was some evidence that the atomic bomb might be built, but there was also a lot of evidence that it wouldn't, but he ultimately decided to try. Because if it were true, the impact would be huge, so it was worth doing.

2015-2017 There was some, and increasingly more, evidence that AI might be a big deal. In 2016, I talked to my mentor: I had done startups and wanted to work on AI security, but my math wasn't strong enough, and I didn't know what to do. At the time, some people said I needed to be proficient in decision theory; some said there wouldn't be any crazy AI events, and there were very few people who truly supported it.

Jack Clark:

In 2014, I was considered crazy for doing ImageNet trend reporting. In 2015, I wanted to write about NVIDIA because all my papers mentioned GPUs, and I was also told I was crazy; in 2016, when I left journalism for AI, I even received an email saying, "You've made the biggest mistake of your life." At the time, from many perspectives, seriously betting on "scalability would succeed" did seem like madness.

Jared Kaplan: How did you decide? Were you conflicted?

Jack Clark: I made a contrarian bet: I asked to be a full-time AI journalist and double my salary, knowing they wouldn't agree. Then I woke up one morning and quit. Because I was reading archives every day, I always felt like something crazy was happening, and at some point you should bet with high conviction. Tom Brown: I wasn't that decisive; I wavered for six months. Daniela Amodei: And back then, the idea that "engineers can significantly advance AI" wasn't mainstream. It was "only researchers could do AI," so your hesitation isn't surprising. Tom Brown: Later, OpenAI said, "You can help make AI safer through engineering," which is why I joined. Daniela, were you my manager at OpenAI? Why did you join? Daniela Amodei: I was at Stripe for five and a half years, and Greg was my boss. I also introduced Greg to Dario. He was starting OpenAI at the time, and I told him, "The smartest person I know is Dario. If you can get him on the team, you'll be very lucky." Later, Dario joined OpenAI. Perhaps like you, I was also thinking about what I wanted to do after leaving Stripe. The reason I joined Stripe was because after working in nonprofits and international development, I felt I needed more skills. Actually, at the time, I thought I would eventually return to that field. Before joining Stripe, I felt I didn't have the capacity to help those less fortunate than myself. So I was looking at other tech companies, hoping to find a new way to make a bigger impact, and OpenAI seemed like a great option. It's a nonprofit organization dedicated to achieving a very important and meaningful goal. I've always believed in the potential of AI, and I knew a bit about Dario, and they definitely needed someone to help manage things, so I felt this job was a perfect fit for my background. I thought, "This is a nonprofit, a group of very talented people with a great vision, but their operations seem a bit disorganized." It was this challenge that excited me, because I could join them. At the time, I felt like a jack-of-all-trades, not only managing team members but also leading technical teams and managing organizational expansion. I was responsible for organizational expansion, worked on the language team, and later took on other tasks. I've also been involved in some policy-related matters and have worked with Chris. I feel there are many talented people in the company, which makes me particularly eager to join and help the company become more efficient and organized. Jack Clark: I remember after GPT-3 you said, "Have you heard of trust and safety?" Daniela Amodei: I used to lead the trust and safety team at Stripe. For technologies like this, you might need to consider the issue of trust and safety. It's actually a bridge between AI Safety Research and more practical day-to-day work—that is, how to make the model truly safe. It's very important to say that "this technology will have a significant impact in the future." At the same time, we also need to do some more practical work on a daily basis to lay the foundation for facing higher-risk scenarios in the future.

Responsible Scaling Policy: Ensuring the Safe Development of AI

Jack Clark: This is a good opportunity to talk about how the Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) came about, why we came up with it, and how we are applying it now, especially considering the work we've done so far regarding model trust and security. So, who first proposed this RSP (Responsible Scaling Policy)?

Dario Amodei:

It was initially proposed by Paul Christiano and me, around the end of 2022. The initial idea was, should we temporarily limit the scaling of the model before it reaches a certain size, until we find a way to address certain security issues?

How to Build a Culture of Trust

Daniela Amodei: I often think about our strategic strengths as a team, and one that might sound surprising but is very important is the high level of trust between us. It's very difficult to get a large group of people to share a common mission, but at Anthropic, we've been able to successfully communicate that sense of mission to an increasing number of people. Within this team, including leadership and all members, everyone is brought together by a shared mission. Our mission is both clear and pure, which is rare in the tech industry.

Jack Clark: So, what are you all excited about about what we're going to do next?

Chris Olah:

I think there are many reasons to be excited about interpretability. One is obviously for safety reasons, but there's another reason, which I think is equally exciting or meaningful on an emotional level, and that's because I think neural networks are incredibly beautiful, and there's a lot of beauty in them that we haven't seen yet. We always treat neural networks as a black box and aren't particularly interested in their internal structure, but when you start to delve into them, you find that they're full of amazing structures.

It's a bit like how people look at biology. Some people might think, "Evolution is boring; it's just a simple process that runs for a long time and then creates animals."

It's a bit like how people look at biology. Some people might think, "Evolution is boring; it's just a simple process that runs for a long time and then creates animals."

It's a bit like how people look at biology. Some people might think, "Evolution is boring; it's just a simple process that runs for a long time and then creates animals."

"But in reality, every animal created by evolution is full of incredible complexity and structure. And I think evolution is an optimization process, like training a neural network. Inside a neural network, there is also a whole complex structure similar to 'artificial biology.' If you are willing to delve into them, you will find many amazing things. I think we are only just beginning to unveil it. It is so incredible, there is so much waiting for us to discover. We have only just begun to open its doors, and I think the discoveries to come will be very exciting and wonderful. Sometimes I imagine walking into a bookstore ten years from now and buying a textbook on the interpretability of neural networks, or a book that really talks about the 'biology' of neural networks, and there will be all sorts of amazing content in it. I believe that in the next ten years, or even the next few years, we will begin to really discover these things, and it will be a crazy and amazing journey." Jack Clark: A few years ago, if someone said, "The government will set up new agencies to test and evaluate AI systems, and these agencies will be very professional and effective." "You might not believe it's true. But it's happening. You could say governments have established a 'new embassy' to deal with this new category of technology, and I'm excited to see where that leads. I think it really means that nations are capable of handling such social transformations, not just businesses, and I'm happy to be a part of it." Daniela Amodei: "I'm already excited about this, but I find it hard not to be excited just imagining what AI can do for humanity in the future. Even the signs that Claude can help develop vaccines, conduct cancer research, and biological research are incredible. It's amazing what it can do now, and I'm incredibly excited when I look three to five years into the future and imagine Claude truly solving many of the fundamental problems facing humanity, especially in the health field. Thinking back to my days working in international development, how amazing it would have been if Claude could have helped me with my inefficient work back then." Tom Brown: Personally, I really enjoy using Claude at work. So, lately I've also been using Claude frequently at home, and the biggest change recently has been in code. Six months ago, I didn't use Claude for any programming-related work, and our team rarely used it for coding either, but that has changed significantly now. For example, last week I gave a presentation at a Y Combinator event. At the beginning, I asked the audience, "How many people use Claude for programming now?" "As a result, almost 95% of people raised their hands. Almost everyone in the room raised their hands, a stark contrast to four months ago." Dario Amodei: "When I think about things that excite me, I think about places I mentioned earlier where there seems to be a consensus, but that consensus is about to be broken, and one of them is explainability. I believe that explainability is not only key to guiding and ensuring the safety of AI systems, but it also encompasses profound insights into intelligent optimization problems and how the human brain works. I've said before that Chris Olah will one day win the Nobel Prize in Medicine." Because I was a neuroscientist, and many of the mental illnesses we haven't solved, such as schizophrenia or mood disorders, I suspect are related to some higher-level systemic problem. However, due to the complexity of the human brain and its difficulty in direct study, these problems are hard to fully understand. While neural networks aren't a perfect analogy, they are not as difficult to analyze and interact with as the human brain. Over time, neural networks will become a better analogy tool." Another relevant area is the application of AI in biology. Biology is an extremely complex subject, and for many reasons, people remain skeptical of it, but I think that consensus of skepticism is beginning to crumble. We've already seen the Nobel Prize in Chemistry awarded to AlphaFold, which is a remarkable achievement, and we should strive to develop tools that can help us create hundreds of "AlphaFolds." Finally, there's the use of AI to enhance democracy. We worry that if AI is developed incorrectly, it could become a tool of authoritarianism. So, how can AI be a tool for promoting freedom and self-determination? I think this area may have started earlier than the previous two, but its importance is no less. Jared Kaplan: I think there are at least two points that echo your earlier points. One is that I think many people joined Anthropic because they have a great curiosity about AI science. With AI... With technological advancements, they have gradually come to recognize that we not only need to drive technological development, but also to understand it more deeply and ensure its security. I find it exciting to work with a growing number of people who share a common vision for AI development and responsibility, and I think many of the technological advancements of the past year have indeed contributed to this consensus. On another front, returning to practical issues, I think we have done a lot of work on AI security. But with recent developments, we have begun to have some initial understanding of the risks that very advanced systems may pose. This allows us to directly study and investigate these risks through interpretability studies and other types of security mechanisms. In this way, we can gain a clearer understanding of the potential risks of advanced AI systems, which will enable us to advance our mission in a more scientific and empirical manner. Therefore, I am very excited about the next six months as we leverage our understanding of the potential problems with advanced systems to further research and find ways to avoid these pitfalls.

data.title: Tether, the leading stablecoin provider, suddenly laid off two gold traders.
data.description:
Stablecoin giant Tether has laid off its gold trading team, poached from HSBC. According to a Bloomberg report on March 31, citing sources familiar with the matter, Tether has dismissed two senior precious metals traders, Vincent Domien and Mathew O'Neill, both of whom joined the company several months ago from HSBC Holdings. The reasons for their departure are unclear. They joined Tether during a record year of gold purchases, exceeding those of almost all central banks. In response to inquiries from Bloomberg News, Tether stated: "The company is committed to maintaining a lean team and continuously optimizing operations." This personnel change comes as Tether faces pressure from a potential correction in gold prices. In March of this year, influenced by the situation in the Middle East, crude oil prices surged, while the gold market experienced its worst monthly performance since 2008. Against this backdrop, crude oil prices denominated in gold rose sharply this month, breaking a multi-year downward trend.

Crude oil prices denominated in gold rose sharply in March

Poaching HSBC is aimed at dominating the gold market

The joining of Dominen and O’Neill initially attracted widespread attention in the gold market. Both Dominen and O'Neill previously held senior positions at HSBC; Dominen was responsible for global metals trading, while O'Neill was a top salesperson. Tether recruited them with the goal of professionalizing its gold reserve management and generating revenue for the company through active gold asset management, even publicly stating its intention to create "the world's best gold trading platform." Tether joined these two shortly after completing a record-breaking year of gold purchases, exceeding the purchases of almost all central banks. However, with gold prices impacted in March by a sell-off triggered by the Iran-Iraq War, rising interest rate expectations, and selling by at least one central bank, the simultaneous decline in gold and Bitcoin has put pressure on its portfolio. Analysts believe that Tether appears to have chosen to shrink its team after completing its initial investment; the company has approximately 300 employees. This round of layoffs also reflects another side of the talent war in the commodities industry. Over the past year, several traders have spared no expense in poaching talent from traditionally dominant banking institutions in the gold market. According to reports, Mercuria Energy Group recruited Benjamin Binet-Laisne from Goldman Sachs, and Gunvor Group also recruited a number of traders from various institutions. The first comprehensive audit has been launched, and financing plans have been put on hold. These personnel changes in the gold team come at a crucial juncture for Tether's own transformation. Tether announced this month that it has commissioned a major accounting firm to conduct its first comprehensive financial audit. At the same time, the company's plan to raise up to $20 billion in external funding has been shelved pending the audit results. Tether is the issuer of USDT, the world's largest dollar-pegged stablecoin, with a current circulating supply of approximately $184 billion. At the beginning of the year, the company held approximately 140 tons of gold, primarily used to back USDT and its smaller gold-pegged stablecoin, XAUT.

data.title: 95-year-old Warren Buffett said 7 things: Now is not the time to buy the dip.
data.description:
Author: Curry｜Deep Tide TechFlow

Yesterday, Buffett gave an exclusive interview to CNBC.

This is the first time he has sat down and talked for so long since stepping down as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway on January 1st of this year. At 95 years old, after 60 years at the helm, and handing over the reins to Greg Abel, one would think he could stop talking.

But he talked about Apple, the Federal Reserve, Bill Gates and Epstein, Iran's nuclear weapons, and also announced that his charity lunch, after a four-year hiatus, will be revived.

There's a lot of information; let's go through it one by one.

I. Going to work on the first day of retirement

Buffett said he still goes to the office every day.

Every day before the market opens, he calls Mark Millard, Berkshire Hathaway's Chief Financial Officer, to discuss market dynamics and trades. Millard's office is about six meters away from his; after the call, he goes to execute the trade.

When asked about recent moves, Buffett said yes, he made a tiny purchase (one lot), but didn't disclose what it was. He also drew a line: he wouldn't make any investments Abel disagreed with, and Abel receives a daily summary of investment developments. This arrangement sounds like "final decision-making power is in the new CEO's hands," but in another way, it also means the 95-year-old predecessor is present, involved, and trading daily, while the successor works six meters away. In the interview, Buffett praised Abel repeatedly, saying Abel does more work in a day than he did in a week at his peak, and that he'd rather have him manage his money than entrust it to any top American investment advisor. In name, he withdrew. But this "withdrawal" is more like going from CEO to someone sitting on the sidelines. II. Sold Apple too early, but won't buy again. In this interview, Buffett admitted one thing: Berkshire Hathaway sold its Apple stake too early. His exact words were: "I sold it too soon. But, I bought it even sooner, so." In other words, he sold early, but bought even earlier, so he still made a profit. He revealed that Berkshire Hathaway's pre-tax profit on this Apple investment exceeded $100 billion. Here's the timeline: Berkshire Hathaway began buying Apple around 2016, and its holdings grew to a peak of over $170 billion, making it Berkshire's largest single holding in history. Between 2023 and 2024, Berkshire reduced its Apple position by about two-thirds. As of the end of last year, according to SEC filings, Berkshire held approximately 22.79 million shares of Apple, worth about $62 billion, still representing 22.6% of its entire portfolio and remaining its largest holding. Warren Buffett said that Apple is better than any company wholly owned by Berkshire. Berkshire Hathaway's railroad company, BNSF, has a market capitalization higher than its Apple holdings, yet Apple remains at the top of its portfolio. He also praised Apple CEO Tim Cook as a "fantastic manager," saying Cook doesn't know how he gets along with everyone in the world. However, when asked if he would increase his holdings, Buffett gave a conditional answer: it's not impossible; if Apple's price drops to a certain level, Berkshire will buy heavily. But not now, "not in this market." Apple has fallen more than 14% from its peak this year, but in Buffett's view, it hasn't reached a price he's willing to sell at.

III. After three 50% drops, what's this now?

US stocks haven't fared well this year. As of the day of the interview, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was down about 5% year-to-date, the S&P 500 was down about 6%, and the Nasdaq was down about 9%. Apple itself has also retreated more than 14% from its year-to-date high.

Buffett's reaction was: There's nothing to be excited about.

He said that since he took over Berkshire Hathaway, the company's stock price has fallen by more than 50% at least three times. Compared to those, this current drop is nothing.

He also didn't show any intention to make a major move.

For him, the current market is far from the historical moment that "created great opportunities." In just a few words, he's saying both not to panic and not to expect to buy at the bottom now. Fourth, the inflation target should be zero. When asked whether the Federal Reserve is more concerned about inflation or employment, Buffett didn't directly choose sides, but said something even more significant: he hopes the Fed's inflation target is zero. His logic is that once you say you can tolerate 2% inflation, that number becomes incredibly significant over the long term with compound interest. Moreover, for ordinary people, if you save money and earn 2% interest, but then have to pay taxes on that 2%, it means your purchasing power is actually declining. Buffett also said that he is more concerned about the dollar's status as the global reserve currency and the stability of the banking system than employment data. He praised Federal Reserve Chairman Powell's decisive action during the outbreak of the pandemic in March 2020, saying that if it had been delayed by two or three weeks, it would have been a disaster, "Once the dominoes start falling, they fall faster than anyone thinks." In Buffett's mind, Powell and Paul Volcker, who tamed inflation with aggressive interest rate hikes in the 1980s, are the two most respectable figures in the history of the Federal Reserve. However, he is not without criticism of the Federal Reserve. He sees the 2% inflation target as a mistake, arguing that it's tantamount to telling everyone that spending money is worse than saving. V. The Charity Lunch is Back! What prompted Buffett to change his mind was the death of one person. Cecil Williams, founder of the Glide Foundation, passed away in 2024. Glide is a San Francisco-based charity that helps the homeless. Buffett's ex-wife, Susan, had been a long-time volunteer there. Starting in 2000, Buffett auctioned off an annual opportunity to dine with him, donating all proceeds to Glide. This lunch was held for 22 years, and after the final event in 2022, Buffett announced it would no longer be held. [Image: The person in the middle is Cecil Williams] But Cecil's death changed his mind. Buffett said that Cecil believed Glide should not die when he passed away. This time, the new lunch has a different format. Warren Buffett will no longer be attending alone; he will be accompanied by four-time NBA champion Stephen Curry and his wife, Ayesha Curry. The auction begins on eBay on May 7th, with a starting bid of $50,000. The winning bidder can bring up to seven guests to Omaha on June 24th for lunch with three people. Half of the proceeds will be donated to Glide, and the other half to the Eat. Learn. Play. Foundation, founded by the Currys, a non-profit organization that helps Oakland youth. The last cryptocurrency-related auction was in 2019, when Tron founder Justin Sun spent $4.57 million. Since then, Buffett has donated the Bitcoin Justin Sun gave him. VI. No Longer in Contact with Gates This is the first time Buffett has publicly spoken about Bill Gates since stepping down. He said he hasn't spoken to Gates since the Epstein documents were released. His exact words were: "I don't want to be in a position where I know things... to be called as a witness." He doesn't want to know too much and doesn't want to be subpoenaed to testify. Buffett and Gates have had a relationship spanning decades. In 2010, they jointly launched the Giving Pledge, calling on the world's richest people to pledge to donate the majority of their wealth to charity. Since 2006, Warren Buffett has donated over $43 billion to the Gates Foundation. Gates' relationship with Epstein began in 2011, three years after Epstein's 2008 plea in Florida for sex crimes. Since the end of last year, the U.S. Department of Justice and Congress have released a large number of related documents, including emails and photos between Gates and Epstein. According to a previous report by the Wall Street Journal, Gates apologized to Gates Foundation employees in February of this year, admitting to his relationship with Epstein and acknowledging an extramarital affair. Gates has accepted an invitation from the U.S. House Oversight Committee to testify on this matter, but the date has not yet been set. When asked if he and Gates were still good friends, Buffett's tone was restrained. He mentioned their past collaboration on the Giving Pledge, but then added: "I don't think it's necessary to say more until things are clear." VII. Nuclear Weapons Will Be Used Sooner or Later. The interview concluded with a discussion of Iran. Buffett said that there are currently nine countries in the world possessing nuclear weapons. During the Cold War, there were only two, the United States and the Soviet Union, and everyone was extremely tense. But back then, the decision-makers on both sides were at least rational. Now, the situation is completely different. He specifically mentioned Iran and North Korea. He said the most dangerous scenario is that the person holding the nuclear button is about to die or facing immense shame. In such a situation, no one can predict what decision a person will make. When asked what advice he would give to the US president on dealing with Iran's enriched uranium, Buffett didn't offer specific advice, but instead said something that sounded fatalistic: Nuclear weapons will be used within the next hundred or perhaps two hundred years. He didn't know exactly how it would happen, but he believed it was a matter of probability, and the more countries possessing nuclear weapons, the higher the probability. Regarding whether Iran should possess nuclear weapons, he only said: If Iran had nuclear weapons, things would be more difficult than if it didn't. A 95-year-old man who witnessed the end of World War II, the entire Cold War, the Cuban Missile Crisis, the collapse of the Soviet Union... His final assessment during the interview was thought-provoking. The interview lasted about an hour, and he covered everything that could be discussed. But the detail that struck me most was this: Buffett, at 95 and three months out of office, didn't utter a single word about "reflecting" or "summarizing his life" in front of the camera. Everything he said was forward-looking. Should he still buy Apple products? How should the Federal Reserve set its goals? How will the situation in Iran develop? What should be done about the new charity lunch?... The host, Becky Quick, sitting across from him, gave him many opportunities to reminisce, but he declined them all. You rarely see someone who has relinquished power still more interested in the future than in the past. Even when the stock market guru is old, he may still be a guru. It's just that before he voted with money, now he votes with his judgment.

data.title: The person who leaked the Claude Code was a young Chinese man who jokingly called himself an amateur.
data.description:
Claude Code's source code has been leaked.

Nearly two thousand files and over half a million lines of TypeScript code have been exposed on the public internet. This is one of the largest accidental public releases of source code among leading AI companies to date.

The developer, Chaofan Shou, was the first to discover and publicly disclose this "vulnerability."

The developer, Chaofan Shou, was the first to discover and publicly disclose this "vulnerability."

Developer @realsigridjin acted swiftly, immediately backing up the leaked source code to GitHub. The repository is named instructkr/claude-code and labeled "Claude Code Snapshot for Research". Within just two hours of its launch, the repository garnered over 50,000 stars, setting a new record for the fastest GitHub repository to reach this milestone.

Here is the Github address: https://github.com/instructkr/claude-code

The incident stemmed from Anthropic mistakenly packaging and releasing the source map debugging files along with a Claude Code version release to a public package repository.

The incident occurred when Anthropic mistakenly packaged and released the source map debugging files to a public package repository during a Claude Code version release.

The source map file exposed the location of the original source code archive, allowing outsiders to access and download the compressed source code package in Anthropic's cloud storage. Anthropic subsequently responded that the incident was a "human error during the packaging process," not a security breach, and that no user data or credentials were leaked. Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, also briefly commented on X, confirming it was simply a "developer error." After the news spread, Musk also joined in the fun, retweeting a post from a netizen jokingly saying that Anthropic is now more open than OpenAI, and leaving a comment saying "That's amazing?". It's worth mentioning that Chaofan Shou, who discovered this leak, is a Chinese security researcher with a very impressive resume. His GitHub username is shouc, and his X account is @shoucccc. According to his LinkedIn profile, he entered the University of California, Santa Barbara in 2019 to pursue a bachelor's degree in Computer Science. He completed his studies in approximately two years, graduating early with a perfect GPA of 4.0. The only note in the degree section is: "too easy".

Some netizens have done the math, which is equivalent to maintaining the intensity of preparing for the college entrance exam every semester and getting the highest score in every subject for three consecutive years.

After graduating with his bachelor's degree, he went to the University of California, Berkeley to pursue a PhD in Computer Science under the tutelage of Koushik Sen, a renowned professor in the field of program analysis. He later chose to drop out and start his own business. His LinkedIn bio is equally concise: "dropped out lol (dropped out, haha)".

He previously worked as a security engineer at Salesforce, where he was responsible for developing static code analysis tools and data pipelines. Later, he joined Veridise to lead the development of automated testing tools for smart contracts. In 2023, he co-founded Fuzzland, a Web3 security and high-frequency trading company, and served as its CTO. The company was later acquired by Solayer, where he became a full-time software engineer. His job description read "full-time larping on Twitter." In short, he was jokingly referring to himself as someone who spends all his time posting on Twitter to gain attention, and mocking himself for not doing his job properly. Fuzzland has helped recover over $30 million in stolen on-chain assets and currently protects and manages over $5 billion in assets. His flagship project on GitHub, ityfuzz, is a snapshot fuzzing tool for smart contracts and has garnered over 1,100 stars. On the academic front, his Google Scholar page shows a total of 224 citations, an h-index of 6, and an i10-index of 4. His papers have been published in top academic conferences such as CCS, ISSTA, and CoNext, and his research interests include smart contract fuzzing, web security, and distributed systems. In the bug bounty field, he has been active since 2016. The vulnerabilities he has disclosed cover well-known platforms such as Twitter, Etherscan, Devin.ai, and Google Nest, involving various high-risk issues such as account hijacking, system takeover, and user behavior leakage. He has received approximately $1.9 million in bug bounties. Over the past year, he has maintained an active pace of 1159 commits on GitHub. After this person's identity sparked widespread discussion on social media, some netizens commented in the comments section: "Is it Shou Chaofan? He's my junior from high school." Public information also seems to confirm this clue: he attended Shanghai Pinghe Bilingual School and was selected as a university publicity officer by the China Computer Federation (CCF). The screenshot is from the WeChat official account "Pinghe Education". Online, there are even teaching materials circulated with his hand-drawn illustrations to help solve AMC 10 problems. Of course, the possibility of someone with the same name cannot be ruled out. Interestingly, Anthropic presents itself as the most security-conscious AI company, and the person who exposed this facade is a Chinese programmer on GitHub who calls himself a "senior script kiddie". "Script kiddie" is a term used in hacker culture to mock those who are technically illiterate and only know how to copy tools without understanding the underlying principles. Posting this phrase prominently on one's homepage is undoubtedly just a joke. But it was precisely this person—perhaps due to professional habit—who, during a casual technical check, revealed Anthropic's 500,000 lines of source code to everyone.
